title: The Macro Minute: Should investors chase the pending wave of trillion-dollar IPOs?

Happy Tuesday out there team42. It's your skipper here, Darius Delivers at our macro minute for Tuesday, June 9th, 2026. As always, we'll start with the executive summary from today's leadoff morning note. So, let's dive right in. Today's key micro question is, should investors chase the pending wave of trillion dollar IPOs? The short answer is, historical data of mega cap tech IPOs suggests long-term investors who have emotional fortitude and/or low liquidity preference required to ride out significant draw downs can do well with the set it and forget it approach. History suggests however that riskaware investors who are emotionally able to sit out the initial euphoria are likely to get a far better price to acquire the shares within the first year of trading. This frequentist probability signal that prudence is likely to be rewarded by the market is consistent with our basian party like its 1998 thesis. Terms of our analytical nuance, SpaceX IPO books are overs subscribed ahead of closing on Wednesday. This comes amid Open AAI confidentially filing for a public listing Monday, becoming the third major AI developer to do so and joining an IPO pipeline now valued at roughly $3.6 trillion. SpaceX has targeted an IPO valuation near 1.8 trillion. Anthropic race capital at 965 billion valuation last month and OpenAI was valued at 852 billion in its March funding round. Bloomberg is reporting that surges in IPO activity have historically preceded lower market returns and compressed price toearnings multiples. Their data show the 30 largest technology IPOs over the past 15 years averaged a maximum draw down of 55% in their first year of trading. Let me read that again. Their data shows the 30 largest technology IPOs over the past 15 years averaged a maximum draw down of 55% within their first year of trading. The average performance of the stock price over the first week and year of trading were 4% and 14% respectively. 17 of the 30 or 57% were down a year from their IPO listing price. So take note of that. As always wrap up with a question from our community. This was titled the 60/40 question. Says new subscriber here says so kisses seems configured as of now to maintain the 60% stocks but replace the 40% bonds with 30% gold and 10% Bitcoin. When I loosely researched the intent of bonds in the traditional 60/40, bonds are supposed to provide a reduced volatility and also income and gold and bitcoin uh doesn't pay a dividend the way bonds do. Also, the signals for gold and bitcoin are currently bearish and no position. So, in this situation, are bonds the replacement or are we sending cash or short-term treasuries until we get the yellow green light to get back in? There's not a lot of questions in options in the fixed income uh sector. So, excellent question that I love geeking out about portfolio construction. This is my core competency. Uh so let's start with the um the 60 the sort of first question uh part of the question which is uh bonds were designed to provide reduced volatility and also income. They certainly provide income, but the point we're making uh on the income side is that income is likely to be negative on a real basis based on the primarily on the choices that the sovereign is likely to make in increasing quantities throughout their duration of this fork turning or whether you want to borrow radalio's big cycle framework until the big cycle uh concludes or if you want to borrow Peter Turin's elite over production framework until this the empire collapses. I don't know what the bright answer is on that. That stuff's too beyond my pay grade. The key takeaway is that based on where we are in this process from an empire building standpoint, from a debt standpoint, and ultimately how we deal with some of these big big geopolitical and domestic political issues, it's highly likely that the central bank and the Treasury, the Treasury and the Fed will continue to do things that reduce the real return on bonds uh uh in a way that is very favorable for the performance of gold and bitcoin. Uh in another way u and as a function of that, you're highly likely to see increased volatility in bonds. We talked about yesterday how we're essentially undergoing a reversal of the great moderation and a reversal of the great moderation is essentially calls for a decline uh in in in excess savings around the world. Recall that we were building excess savings in a way that caused bond prices uh to go persistently up and yields to go persistently down. It allowed inflation to go down. We had an excess and abundance of supply. Right now we have a a deficit of supply across many critical industries uh and resources in the economy and that is obviously being taxed uh in a massive way by AI at the current juncture. So that's orthogonal in addition to everything we're talking about from a geopolitical uh standpoint. So the reduced volatility and income that bonds have provided in the great moderation era when they were a perfect diversifier for stocks is no longer true. And another thing that's no longer true is the fact that bonds are now positively correlated with stocks which also increases the the the volatility of your overall portfolio when you have uh positively correlated assets. So bonds are not a good divers portfolio diversifier. We've been saying this for years. U many Wall Street firms, many of the largest wealth management platforms across global Wall Street followed us in pivoting out of bonds and into gold. Uh we were among the first to break the 6040 portfolio on Global Wall Street and now we're seeing it now now it's now it's now everybody's doing it. So that's how you know we had a good idea there. And then the finally on the income thing I will say um just in terms of bonds paying a dividend. So KISS is designed when it raises cash to park the money in income generating assets on the cash side of things. Right now KISS currently uses um floating rate treasuries which are currently yielding about 4% uh in the cash. So when gold and bitcoin aren't you know generating capital appreciation then kiss will generate income in the form of uh interest rate payments uh in the form of uh fixed income coupons on the short end of the treasury curve. However, we have I know we have many customers particularly on the investment advisor side of things uh in our port probe community uh that are using you know when when KISS raises cash they'll go to something like uh uh uh uh bank loans if we're in a risk on market regime they'll go to something like uh MUN bonds or or or a you know the the core fixed income aggregate if we're in a riskoff market regime you know they'll be a little bit more dynamic in terms of how uh their their customized versions of KISS for their clients uh uh raise cash so that's those are interesting thoughts there consult our Kiss FAQ if you want some um some ideas on how to customize KISS for your risk tolerance and and strategic investment objectives. And then the final thing I'll say is that um you know there's this view that you know gold [clears throat] and bitcoin don't pay dividends therefore bonds are are better assets. Uh just a reminder on a total return basis which factors in the dividends of the the the coupons since we did that in and when we first uh outlined KISS in and introduced Kiss in January 2023 Bitcoin has returned 188% and TLT is down 8% on a total return basis coupons included. Uh since we included gold in KISS we placed a 30% target allocation to agg uh with gold uh uh in Kiss in September of 2024. uh gold is up 55% uh versus TLT being down 1% on a total return basis. So uh don't forget that when bond prices go down, the decline in the bond price can actually erode your entire coupon payment. And so you're sitting there collecting quote unquote dividends while at the same time your portfolio was going down in value. If I were you, I would much prefer a portfolio that is going up in value to the tune of 188% in Bitcoin or up 55% uh in gold. And obviously we're [clears throat] not long those things at the current juncture. over clipping the coupon which is the whole point of the risk management overlays and kiss. So we'll wrap it up there. Jerry Stale here presenting our macro minute for Tuesday, June 9th, 2026. Best of luck out there today. We'll catch you back here tomorrow. Cheers.
